original_abstract:
Higher education is a fundamental driver of socio-economic development, knowledge diffusion, and national competitiveness. While Ethiopia has shifted from an elitist educational model to one characterized by rapid massification over the last two decades, this expansion has raised critical concerns regarding the quality of educational services. This study sought to evaluate the persistent challenges facing Ethiopian higher education and examine the efficacy of government-led quality improvement initiatives. Adopting a qualitative research design, the study utilized document analysis, interviews, and focus group discussions. A comprehensive systematic review was conducted on 89 sources, including legislative policy documents, peer-reviewed journals, doctoral dissertations, and empirical studies spanning from 2004 to 2021. 

The findings indicate that despite the establishment of regulatory bodies such as the Higher Education Relevance and Quality Agency (HERQA) and the implementation of various reform programs, these efforts have had a negligible impact on actual institutional quality. Primary obstacles include a severe shortage of experienced academic staff, inadequate Information Communication Technology (ICT) infrastructure, and a lack of essential physical resources such as laboratories, workshops, libraries, and classrooms. Furthermore, student welfare is hindered by substandard dormitory and administrative services. The study concludes that the misalignment between breakneck expansion and resource allocation has resulted in poor service delivery across most public universities. To address these systemic gaps, it is recommended that the Ethiopian government prioritize human and material resource development, strengthen internal quality assurance mechanisms, and conduct rigorous institutional readiness assessments before further expanding the higher education sector.
humanized_abstract:
Higher education is crucial to socio-economic development, knowledge dissemination and global competitiveness. Over the last two decades Ethiopia has moved from elite higher education to mass higher education in a very fast pace, thereby posing concerns in terms of quality of education provided. Thus, this study aims at identifying the perennial problems affecting higher education in Ethiopia, as well as assessing the effectivness of the policy measures undertaken by the Ministry of Education (MOE). Using qualitative research approach, documentary analysis, interviews and focus group discussions have employed. A systematic review method was used for analyzing 89 literature sources comprising of MOE policy statements and regulations, research articles, PhD theses and studies conducted on higher education in Ethiopia from 2004 to 2021.

Ethiopia: Quality of Higher Education Institutions Found to be “Unchanged” The Bologna Process in Central, Eastern and Southern Europe (BP CESESE) country in focus reports provide an analysis of progress with respect to the Bologna Process. The report describes the development of higher education in Ethiopia, identifies the main challenges facing this sector, describes the main higher education reform initiatives implemented and analyses the effectiveness of these reforms, concluding by providing policy recommendations. The government has taken steps to ensure the higher education system is regulated effectively. For instance, HERQA was put in place to ensure that higher education institutions maintain the required quality of learning and teaching environments. The different reforms also attempted to enhance the overall quality of higher education. But according to the study, the general situation regarding the quality of higher education institutions remains unchanged. This study establishes that despite presence of HERQA and implementing reforms in the higher education sector, the overall quality of higher education institutions was not improved, primarily due to lack of highly qualified academic staff, inadequate ICT facilities, insufficient equipment for laboratories, workshops, libraries and adequate number of class rooms and poor dormitory and administrative services. Accordingly, the rushed expansion of higher education sector in Ethiopia, together with the mismatch between increased intake capacity and the corresponding investments in the necessary infrastructure, have resulted in a generally poor quality of service being offered at most public universities. Thus, the Government should undertake efforts to increase the supply of staff and physical capital for higher education, develop an efficient internal quality assurance mechanism that enables HERQA to give accreditation on stronger grounds, carry out an assessment of the institutional readiness of new higher education institutions prior to their recognition, and proceed with caution when increasing the overall capacity of higher education institutions.
